what_it_does: Describes a projection adapter layer that exposes multiple coexisting read-only UI surfaces over the same kernel substrate, requiring every surface selection to be liftable back to a structural fibre.
observations: The kernel is described as having no single UI; instead it has a projection registry for multiple simultaneous reads over the same substrate.
A projection is valid only if a visible item can lift back through projection item and structural fibre to a substrate address, event, candidate, or entry.

Version 0.12 introduces kernel registry/surface files and core types such as ProjectionAdapter, ProjectionItem, SurfaceSelection, StructuralFibre, ProjectionSurfaceState, VisualAxisField, and ProjectionRegistry.
Nine surface roles are defined: visual axis field, radial graph, parametric box, card board, query table, canvas, text note, timeline, and AI block jobs.

Each surface has a specific role, e.g., visual axis field is the primary orientation instrument, parametric box is local-root mode, canvas is spatial/schema exploration, AI block jobs is automation mode.

The extension law requires a new plugin or visual mode to implement project(substrate) -> ProjectionItem[] and lift(surfaceSelection) -> StructuralFibre and to declare read/write surfaces, gestures, active schema, multiplicity behaviour, and open seams.
A list of current open seams is provided, covering visual-axis grammar, schema-to-colour/shape/motion mapping, layout conventions, round trips for cards/tables/canvas/timeline, and AI conformance.
The operating rule states that any surface that cannot lift back is not a substrate-faithful projection, even if it is a convenient UI.
standouts: The round-trip lift requirement is used as the validity criterion for a projection surface.
AI block jobs are treated as a first-class projection surface with explicit boundary, input addresses, context envelope, and allowed output type.
The extension law frames every new UI mode as a reversible adapter rather than a model owner.
conventions: Markdown implementation note with a status line at the top.
Uses ASCII diagrams for data-flow relationships.
Names core types in PascalCase and registry files in kebab-case JSON filenames.
Ends with an explicit operating rule and an open-seams inventory.
Mixes architectural principles with version-specific filenames and type names.
concerns: Marked as a working-grade implementation note, so details may be provisional.
Declares many open seams without indicating priority, ownership, or resolution criteria.
The strong validity rule has no described enforcement mechanism or test contract in this file.
Core terms such as structural fibre, substrate address, and active schema are used but not formally defined here.
notes: A design note that reframes the kernel UI as a set of substrate-faithful projections, each required to round-trip back to a structural fibre.
questions: What is the exact runtime contract and serialization for a structural fibre?
How is the active schema bound to colour, shape, and motion mappings?
What does multiplicity preservation versus collapse mean concretely for each surface?
How do projection-registry.json and projection-surfaces.json relate to one another at runtime?
How is AI result conformance verified against the allowed output type and boundary?
What happens when a surface cannot lift back: is it rejected, degraded, or merely flagged?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to make interface direction explicit through substrate-faithful projections; the file matches this by defining surfaces, types, an extension law, and open seams.
apparent_themes: multi-surface coexistence over a single substrate
round-trip traceability from surface selection to structural fibre
separation of source model from projected views
explicit surfacing of unresolved design seams
bounded automation surfaces with declared boundaries
intention_signals: It is trying to establish a UI architecture in which every view is a reversible projection over one kernel model, preventing visual tools from silently forking or owning the underlying substrate.
novelty: It treats conventional UI modes (cards, tables, canvas, timeline, AI jobs) as uniform projection surfaces governed by a single lift-back-to-fibre contract, rather than as independent model owners.
contribution: Provides a shared vocabulary (projection, surface, structural fibre, lift) and an extension contract that lets new UI modes be added without claiming ownership of the substrate, while making round-trip fidelity gaps explicit.
summary_for_embedding: Design note for a projection adapter layer that maps a shared kernel substrate into multiple coexisting UI surfaces and requires each surface selection to lift back to a structural fibre.
intention_for_embedding: To define a substrate-faithful, multi-surface UI architecture with an explicit project/lift contract and a registry of projection surfaces.
